attributes.stem: Recent cancer genome sequencing studies have revealed numerous mutations in genes encoding epigenetic regulatory proteins. These mutations lead to widespread alterations in cancer cell epigenomes that contribute to the malignant phenotype.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the relationship between genetic and epigenetic alterations in cancer?
options: Epigenetic alterations primarily affect non-coding regions of the genome
Genetic mutations in chromatin modifiers cause aberrant gene expression
Histone modifications and DNA methylation changes occur independently
Epigenetic changes primarily affect genes involved in metabolic pathways
DNA methylation patterns remain stable despite genetic mutations
attributes.explanation:
Genetic mutations in chromatin modifiers cause aberrant gene expression is the correct answer. Cancer genome sequencing has identified numerous mutations in genes encoding epigenetic regulatory proteins (such as DNA methyltransferases, histone-modifying enzymes, and chromatin remodellers). These genetic alterations lead to widespread changes in the cancer cell epigenome, including abnormal DNA methylation patterns and altered histone modifications. This relationship demonstrates how genetic defects can manifest as epigenetic dysregulation, contributing to the cancer phenotype through aberrant gene expression patterns.

The incorrect options can be explained as follows:

  • Epigenetic alterations primarily affect non-coding regions of the genome - While epigenetic changes do occur in non-coding regions, they also significantly impact coding regions, particularly the promoters of tumour suppressor genes. The distribution of epigenetic alterations is not limited to non-coding regions but occurs throughout the genome in patterns that contribute to oncogenesis.
  • Histone modifications and DNA methylation changes occur independently - These epigenetic mechanisms are actually highly interconnected and often work in concert. For example, histone methyltransferases can recruit DNA methyltransferases, and methyl-binding proteins can recruit histone deacetylases, creating a coordinated epigenetic regulatory network.
  • Epigenetic changes primarily affect genes involved in metabolic pathways - While metabolic genes can be affected by epigenetic alterations, cancer-associated epigenetic changes impact genes across multiple cellular processes, including cell cycle regulation, DNA repair, apoptosis, and differentiation. Tumour suppressor genes are particularly susceptible to epigenetic silencing.
  • DNA methylation patterns remain stable despite genetic mutations - This is incorrect as genetic mutations, particularly in epigenetic regulators like DNMT3A or TET2, directly alter DNA methylation patterns. Global hypomethylation and focal hypermethylation are hallmarks of cancer epigenomes that often result from underlying genetic alterations.

The epigenetic alterations in cancer cells can be broadly categorised into: silencing of tumour suppressor genes by local hypermethylation (e.g., CDKN2A locus encoding p14/ARF and p16/INK4a), global changes in DNA methylation patterns (often associated with mutations in DNA methyltransferases), and alterations in histone positioning or modifications that affect gene expression.
learningPoints: Cancer genomes frequently contain mutations in genes encoding epigenetic regulatory proteins, which can lead to widespread alterations in cancer cell epigenomes.
Epigenetic alterations in cancer include silencing of tumour suppressor genes by DNA hypermethylation, global changes in DNA methylation patterns, and modifications to histones.
Mutations in histone genes themselves can act as driver mutations in some cancers, highlighting the critical role of epigenetic regulation in cancer development.
references: Kumar V, Abbas AK, Aster JC. Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Philadelphia: Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 7; pages 319-320.
